sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Elbow, wrist, and hand pain in FIFO and occupational settings commonly reflects overload, tendinopathy, nerve irritation, or minor trauma, but fracture, tendon rupture, infection, or compartment problems must not be missed.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Localised pain, swelling, grip weakness, or reduced dexterity
- Pain with gripping, lifting, tool use, typing, or impact
- Possible numbness or tingling in median, ulnar, or radial distributions
- Stiffness, clicking, or reduced ability to bear weight through the hand

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Escalate for significant trauma, deformity, tendon rupture, open injury, progressive neurological deficit, severe swelling, infection features, or vascular compromise
- Distinguish tendinopathy from nerve entrapment and from acute fracture patterns
- Repetitive tasks and tool vibration may be major contributors in camp settings

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen mechanism, neurovascular status, skin integrity, and immediate functional loss
- Use support, unload aggravating tasks, and apply simple pain relief strategies within scope
- Escalate when grip loss, marked swelling, deformity, or major neurological symptoms are present

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Perform focused assessment of tendon, joint, and nerve involvement
- Modify tasks such as gripping, lifting, typing, and vibration exposure
- Use graded loading and tendon-specific or nerve-friendly education rather than prolonged immobilisation unless clinically indicated
- Refer for imaging, splinting review, injection discussion, or surgical opinion where rupture, fracture, or persistent compression is suspected

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Worksite tool use, gloves, repetitive force, and awkward wrist postures should be documented because they influence recovery and return-to-work planning
